sources_exist.note: All 11 URL-bearing citations resolve to substantive pages.

figures_match_sources.note: Seven audits fail: the cited pages do not substantiate the active-consolidator count, the claimed paid-multiple median, the 30% material-use baseline, the historical adoption figures and timeline, the 14x retail-platform exit multiple, or the licensing and pricing claims as stated.

judgment_inputs_plausible.note: Several high-impact inputs are presented as sourced observations but are actually unsupported extrapolations, notably active_consolidators=30, buy_mult=9.5, current_adoption_pct=30%, and exit_mult=14x.

rubric_consistent.note: The dataset_inputs chain exactly matches the supplied derived dataset; role labor shares sum to 1.0; contributions sum to ai_replaceable_share 0.39649; V, C, A, B, M, and the geometric-mean S recompute to the stored values within tolerance.

cross_checks_honest.note: The record explicitly treats the code as heterogeneous, addresses uplift versus adoption, assigns the terminal-value class with segment reasoning, and keeps M&A evidence out of V and A. Those cross-check narratives are internally candid despite the citation defects in several underlying inputs.

source_audits.note: The OPTIS article reports 695 deals, identifies leading buyers, and says PE-backed/hybrid buyers dominated, but it does not report a 72% transaction share or an OPTIS table showing 25-35 active PE-backed platforms. The value 30 is an unverified extrapolation.

The page gives indicative ranges of 7-9x for $2-10M revenue firms and 9-12x for $10-50M revenue firms, but it does not provide the median multiple actually paid for $1-10M EBITDA firms. Taking 9.5x across two revenue tiers is an analyst construction, not a cited transaction median.

The article says 31% are not using AI, 33% are just experimenting, and 8% have AI embedded in daily workflows. It does not report the asserted 22% using AI in limited areas, so 22% + 8% = 30% is not supported by this citation.

This page reports a 19.4x average pro forma EBITDA multiple for 2025 deals involving delegated-authority firms such as MGAs and MGUs. It does not state that retail brokerage platform firms averaged 14x at closing or 19x with earn-outs.

The product-oriented article explains comparative-rater benefits but contains neither the claimed 57% agency adoption figure nor the claimed greater-than-90% AMS penetration, and it supplies no 10-15-year time-to-majority history.
The article supports the quoted survey results: 40% of principals were older than 60, 20% older than 65, and half of agencies expected a major ownership change within ten years.

The page supports the core distribution economics used in pi_dist: carrier-paid commissions as a percentage of premium, recurring renewal commissions, independent-agency book ownership, and possible commission leakage through agency, producer, and aggregator splits.

The page corroborates that independent agents generally earn carrier commissions calculated as percentages of premium, including recurring P&C renewal commissions, and discusses direct appointments and profit-sharing.

The Big I article supports persistent independent-agent channel strength and the commercial-lines moat: 62% of all P&C and nearly 88% of commercial-lines premium was placed through independent agencies, with most major lines steady or growing.

The NIPR page is a licensing-management portal and confirms state-issued licenses and state-specific requirements, but it does not establish that every producer must be licensed or that AI cannot legally bind coverage or advise in most states.

The page supports 10-15% new-business and 8-12% renewal commission benchmarks, but not the claim that more than 90% of core retail-agency revenue is commission, that fees are only a small minority, or the quoted blanket statement that contingents add 2-5% of total premium to agency revenue.

reasons: Replace active_consolidators=30 with a count directly documented for the specified buyer class and 24-month window; the OPTIS article does not supply that count or the quoted 72% share.

Use a source reporting the median multiple actually paid for $1-10M EBITDA agencies. The 9.5x input is an analyst midpoint assembled from two different revenue-size ranges, not a cited transaction median.
Correct current_adoption_pct and t50_years from the underlying ACT categories. The cited article reports 8% embedded and 33% experimenting but does not report the asserted 22% limited-area cohort.

Replace the historical analog citation with evidence that contains both the adoption levels and elapsed time. The First Connect page contains none of the quoted 57%, greater-than-90%, or 10-15-year figures.

Replace exit_mult=14x with a directly comparable retail-brokerage platform transaction statistic. The cited MarshBerry page concerns delegated-authority firms and reports 19.4x, not 14x base and 19x with earn-out.

Support the claimed legal moat with primary state licensing or regulator evidence; the generic NIPR licensing portal does not establish the record's universal producer requirement or AI-specific legal conclusion.

Narrow the pricing_structure claim to figures actually on the Agentero page or add evidence for the asserted greater-than-90% revenue share, fee minority, and blanket 2-5% contingent-commission statistic.
